concerns.text: FR-005 mandates a 'paired t-test on 5-fold cross-validation scores' to compare Morgan vs. MACCS. This is methodologically unsound for this design. The two models are trained on DIFFERENT feature spaces (2048-bit vs 166-bit vectors). A paired t-test requires paired observations from the SAME underlying distribution or a direct 1:1 mapping of errors on the SAME test instances. While the test instances are the same, the 'scores' are not paired in a way that isolates the fingerprint effect from the variance of the specific random split. More critically, comparing two models on the SAME test set using a t-test on CV scores often violates the independence assumption of the t-test (the folds are not independent). The standard rigorous approach for comparing two models on the same dataset is a corrected resampled t-test (Nadeau & Bengio) or a non-parametric test like Wilcoxon signed-rank on the test set predictions, not a standard paired t-test on CV scores which inflates Type I error.

The Edge Case 'Similarity Leakage' logic is circular and invalidates the validation target. It states: 'If the Tanimoto similarity threshold (<0.85) results in a test set with fewer than 20 samples, the system must relax the threshold to <0.90'. This creates a data-dependent selection bias. By relaxing the threshold to ensure a minimum sample size, the system is effectively selecting a test set that is MORE similar to the training set. This artificially inflates the performance metrics (ROC-AUC) because the model is being tested on structural analogs it has likely 'seen' in the training distribution. The validation target (performance on the test set) is no longer independent of the training process; it is a function of the relaxation logic. This makes the 'statistical significance' claim (FR-005) invalid as the test set is no longer a true held-out set.

FR-004 specifies an '80/20 stratified split with a Tanimoto similarity threshold of <0.85'. This is a 'scaffold split' or 'similarity split' implementation. However, the spec does not define the algorithm for achieving this. A simple random 80/20 split followed by a check is insufficient; it requires an iterative or greedy algorithm to ensure the 20% test set contains NO compounds with Tanimoto > 0.85 to ANY compound in the 80% train set. If the implementation simply filters the dataset first and then splits, it may fail to meet the constraint. The spec must clarify that the split is a 'maximal dissimilarity split' or 'scaffold split' to ensure the independence of the validation target from the training data.

SC-003 states: 'The feature importance distribution is measured against the hypothesis that Morgan bits surrounding the phosphorus center are more predictive...'. This is a hypothesis, not a measurable outcome. Success criteria should measure the *result* (e.g., 'The top 10 most important bits in the Morgan model are mapped to the phosphorus center substructure'), not the hypothesis itself. The hypothesis is the research question; the SC should be the metric used to answer it.
